transcript.transcript: A barber that I served my apprenticeship with, he served his apprenticeship with the Fort Sherman barber. And he used to tell me about, he told me one time about a situation. He came here in 1910, and he worked with this barber who had been at the fort. And he had a barber, at that time he had a barber shop, and he would go to the old shop. And he said that this barber's daughter was married to a guy at the fort. And about once a week he would take a walk. And she didn't think anything of this, but he'd go take his walk. And then sometimes it'd be twice a week. And finally her curiosity got the best of her, so she followed him one night. And she followed him to a deserted wagon. And he crawled up in the wagon and waited. And another woman eventually came to the wagon, too. And they both got inside the covered wagon. And then she realized that there was a little hanky-panky going on. So about a week or so later, she waited for him to take another walk. And this time, she followed him again. And when both parties had covered themselves inside the wagon, why, she threw about three sticks of dynamite underneath the wagon and blew them up. And they said that parts of their body was dropping out of the trees down there. And this situation took place on River Avenue, which is right near the junior college. And this is one situation that we were taught. And then later on, I understand that she had a nervous breakdown and they had to put her in a sanitarium. But I always like to tell that story because there's a moral there that says, hell hath no fury like a woman scorned. I didn't use that. But that's one of them. I mean, those are the kind of things that, the kind of yarns I've been picking up from time to time on down there. But It's hard to remember off the top of your head what used to happen, but these are the kind of things that I always like to look into. And then, of course, there were people who used to go down there to trade. A lot of people didn't realize this, but the Indians used to stay on Black Ball Island and row across the river there and trade for bacon and trade their furs and things like that for bacon and things like that, or whatever money they could have. That used to be a real trading center for a while. Did the Indians do much trapping around here? Oh, yes, they did. That was one of their big mainstays. Then they sold their furs to the American Fur Company, and to the representatives of the American Fur Company. One of those people was Steve Liberty, for whom Liberty Lake was named. And his great-grandson is a dentist here in Coeur d'Alene. His name is Dr. Seagrave. So if you check with the Seagrave family, you'll probably get more information about liberty and his activity here. And the American fur company ran right out of Fort Sherman? Well, the representatives were here, just like they were all over the Northwest. They had trading posts or districts that people could come to them for information or get the prices on different kinds of furs, things like that. What was the reasoning for opening that fort? Do you have any idea? Right here in Coeur d'Alene. Well, the main reason was because of the Nez Perce wars. They were afraid that the Coeur d'Alene Indians might take part in the Nez Perce wars, and they felt that it might be a wise idea to station somebody here to kind of keep an observation of them. See, the group at Fort Wright was what was more or less checking on the Spokane Indians, and so they needed somebody here to kind of keep track of what the movement of the Coeur d'Alene Indians. Actually, it was just a security measure more than anything else. And then, of course, there were a lot of people starting to move in here by 1880 there were 523 people in in Kootenai County and There so there's a lot of these homesteaders were looking to the Army for protection. So naturally, they would feel better if there was an army or somebody they could call. And they only had one sheriff. And he lived in Rathbun. And he was Tom Brophy. And he was a territorial sheriff, and he couldn't get around to everybody. So consequently, it was important that they had the Army do this to take care of the protection. When did the army first come in there? I mean, it had to have been before they built the fort. Do you know when they first established camp? Well, according to the records, I understand about 1876, they first took on their lease, first settled on the Spokane River right there. And then I believe it was about later on why General Sherman authorized to build another fort then. I'm wondering about that. When did the transition, this is a question of my own, When was the transition between the blue to the brownish-green uniform? I think that came just before the First World War. I think somebody told me that. I wondered about that, too, And they said it didn't happen until after the turn of the century. What was their, what was most of the activities then, on the fort? Well, outside of their customary parties, they used to have, in the summertime, they usually had dances, special dances, once or twice a month for officers and enlisted men. And then there was, of course, actually there were their church socials. There was one or two to the Catholic church. And then the Fort Sherman Chapel was there. And they were groups of people that functioned through the churches. Then there was the parades. There was always the Fourth of July parade, which I've got a picture of that, the Fourth of July parade, and also the band concert. I've got a picture of band concerts where they used to play in 1887. They're playing the Anvil Chorus down where J.C. Penny's parking lot is now. And then there was usually in the wintertime, they had skating off of Tubbs Hill. When the lake froze over, they did a lot of skating. And of course, there was all kinds of winter sports in the wintertime. They used to have horses and sleigh rides and things like that. But generally speaking, some of the... I remember one fellow telling me that When he was there before why? Every Saturday they would they would load up a wagon Of men and they would go to Spokane and there used to be where the old Coeur d'Alene Hotel is, there used to be a big saloon there. And there was a big dance floor down at the bottom, and a bar across the back. And then up above, along the sides were these cages. And the sporting women used these rooms to accommodate their paying customers and the they danced down there and they the fellows would buy them drinks. This fellow told me an experience he had. He was only 17 years old, but he looked a lot older. So the guys took him in with him. He met one of these sporting women. And he fell in love with her. And He wanted to marry her. He kept going back and seeing her. Every time he could get away, he'd go and see her. Finally, he proposed to her, wanted to marry her, and she started crying. She said that she would understand. He wouldn't understand that, But in a few years, he'd learned to hate her. So she said that it would be better that they just set and not see each other anymore. She was about 30 years old. And like I said, he was about 17, and she thought he was about 21 or 22. And so anyway, he said the next time he went in there, why, he couldn't find her. And they said that she'd asked for a transfer, and she went on to Seattle. She moved on and went to Seattle. Apparently, things were getting a little bit too serious for her, too, and she felt for us, for her own sake as well as his, we'd better just get out of town. The chapel, the little red chapel over there, is that a Methodist chapel Or is that Baptist? No, I think it was Episcopal. I wouldn't say for sure, but it seems to me the Episcopal Church had something to do with that. Because I've talked to members of the Episcopal Church and they said that they took care of some of the altars and things like that, they fixed the altar, so I assume they must have had something to do with it. You mentioned the Catholic religion over there. Was that pretty strong on that on that fort? Well, yes it was really because you see Father, let's see what was his name, the first, you know before Father Smith, He built a little cabin down there at the foreground. And Father Point, I think his name was. And he had His first church was down there, but that was before the Fortesville, and I think that was in about 18 What was it I Can't want to say 1846, but I'm not sure but anyway I understand that he ministered to the Indians then and then of course the water Came up on the river and wiped out his cabin. So he moved Moved over on st. Mary's and then they went from there to present one. I wish I knew more of the facts on that. I remember reading that. Mrs. Segraves had written something on it that was really interesting. She'd done quite a lot of research on it, quarterly in Indian history. But he moved down to St. Marys and he was one of them that made the move up to? That's what I understand. The levee there, when Did they build that levee? Was that built to keep the fort from getting flooded? No, no, that was built back in the 30s, 18, 1930s That was built when they had the last big flood in 1933 and the whole area was flooded. The park was, the city park was under two feet of water, too. And that water came down. See, visualize this lake as just like, it's a big bottle. The only trouble is there's two big rivers draining into it and only one river draining out of it. So consequently, all of your water pressure builds up at the mouth of the Spokane River. And naturally, it spills over and backs up. This is what we're faced with. Someday, it's going to happen again. The junior college is going to be in serious trouble, too, if they ever get a winter like we've had sometimes. Yeah, we've had pretty mild winters. Yeah, they've been real mild, really, Really, really true. If we ever get a lot of snow some winter, followed by a lot of rain, there'll be a lot of trouble down there. How'd that fort hold up during the winter? They had a road came across Somewhere around the Cedars, was it? Where they went across the river? How'd they get across that river to get to the port? Well, before that dike was built, that water was only belly deep to a horse. You could drive on that sandbar on the point there, you could drive a horse and wagon across there after the runoff, the water runoff that went over the falls. A lot of people used to say, I remember a fellow told me that his dad used to tell him about how his mother's grandmother used to go shopping and to drive across the Spokane River right there about where the Northwest Timber is with a team of horses and buy her groceries. The bus board would still be out of the water when they got to come across the river. So that dam made a great big difference? Yeah, the dam was what really made the difference in the depth of the river. They had a lot of dugout canoes around here and it came before Cindy. Yeah, as a matter of fact I was talking to Tommy Callister. He does a lot of diving and we were looking, we come in to see me about some An old boat that went down with some silver. I told him according to my information where it was So he was he was looking over there. He found six Indian boats In that same vicinity. It just looked like a terrific storm must have swept those people all over and sunk their boats right there. But he said all those canoes were laying in about 80 to 90 feet of water. So he said it must have been an over-the-roof storm that swept and swamped those people. He took them and he said it probably happened about 1840, 1850, something like that. And they used to sink those, didn't they whenever they weren't using them as dugouts to keep them swallowed up. Yeah Of course how we they sunk all the stern wheelers to as part of fourth of July celebration They burn them up. They Set them on fire on the 4th of July and watch them burn. That's terrible. Well, I would love to have one of those today Well, the only thing is you could who could afford to take keep them up There's no money available to keep them up even now even if we had one We wouldn't have anybody who would have enough money to maintain them so we could use them or Keep out keep them up for show That cost a lot of money, too Yeah So they had quite a beach out there in front of that fort then, I guess. Oh, yeah, it was, really. It was a nice beach. Just lots of sand there. The fishers, I've seen, have the fort coming in from the other side. In the Puyallup Islands, they used to fish there a lot, too. The Indians used that for their fishing grounds. It's still real good fishing in the spring of the year when the runoff is on. Right there at the mouth. Mm-hmm. Oh, yeah. We go out there and drop. Oh, when I was a kid, we used spark plugs and throw them out, weight our lines down with spark plugs, and use a hanging line and pick up some nice light trout there about 12 to 20 inches long. You can still do that if you know what you're doing. You have to learn how to do these days still. Looking at the fort, going into it, I noticed they had a big hospital right there where the field is now, the baseball field. Yeah, in 1909, they moved that up to the Catholic Church. And that's where the nuns used that as their sanctuary. They lived in that for many years until they built this present church. It was the present one that they had now. It took them almost all summer to move that thing. They were dragging it across on logs, the whole building. It went up, let's see, Indiana Avenue, all the way up Indiana Avenue to the present site. It was on 10th and Indiana Avenue. Did they use any of the buildings out of that fort when they degraded it? I understand some people did buy it. Several people bought it and used them for their own use. Some of them used them for barns. Some used them for dwelling, living dwellings, too. But they're all gone now. I don't think there's any of them left now, because I'm pretty sure there is need in there now. I've noticed the officers quarters, I think it is, they're just letting it waste away. They say it's supposed to be under federal protection. But the college is just letting it waste away. I can't believe it. I hope they don't because they can get grants for historical preservation to keep it up if they want to. I understand that the guy that's running it right now, he'd rather put something out there, like a parking lot. Well, they do have a problem. They've got a lot of buildings. What little parking they got for them. Well, They should hang on. There's a little bit that we have left. They did try to fix up the Oxford's Barracks, I think it was, the big white building. They put a new roof on it. It's still in pretty good shape. When did the fort close down? 1901, originally, officially closed down. That was after the boys went to the Spanish-American War in 1898. And then those that were discharged came back here and came to Spokane and were discharged off Spokane. But they officially closed it in the 19th. What was the reasoning for it? Well, like everything else, there was no need for it anymore. It didn't serve any purpose anymore. There just wasn't enough military activity around here to meet it. I see. And that's when they depleted all the buildings and moved them out? Right. And then it sat there for years until junior colleagues decided they wanted to use it at their college. I think a lot of people that would have taken it were afraid of the flooding dangers that would be involved. So I think that's why a lot of them never got into it. HURST. Fort flooded quite a bit, huh? CHOP. Oh, yeah. Every winter you could expect it. I understand that when Fatty Carroll had three saloons and one of them was down there where the Northwest Timber was, And then he had another saloon about where Wilson's drugstore is now. And when they dug the foundation for that building that Wilson's drugstore is in now, they uncovered five soldier bodies and three Indian bodies. And they figured that these people were killed in and around the saloon and then buried underneath the saloon. And then they were listed as deserters. And then this other notorious saloon that he had was on the river there where the Northwest Timber was. And the stagecoach driver between Fort Sherman and Rathbun told me that his daughter told me that that Fatty had a broom that was out over the river. And if anybody gave him any trouble, they took him up in that room. And then they dropped him through the trap door. And then they went into the river and they were never seen or heard of again. Did they ever do anything to this man? Well they finally figured that it was just too many people disappearing too conveniently so they for a whole year they made his saloons off-limits and then and then finally they he was asked to leave town. Was it the government that asked him to leave? Well, it was a combination of everything because he was bringing in a lot of sporting women. A lot of people don't realize that between 1895 and 1915, we had between 60 and 70 sporting women on the street here in Coeur d'Alene. And first of all, they were accommodating the fellows at the fort. Later on, they were accommodating the lumberjacks that came here. So the town got to be pretty undisciplined and pretty wild, so they had to start cleaning it up when they made some drastic moves to do it. They did quite a bit of gambling here, didn't they? Oh, yeah, they did. I was surprised to see in the paper the other day some guy thinks that we should That suggested that the mayor of Spokane. I believe I said something about They would like to They would like to secede from Spokane would like to secede from that of the Western Washington. And they know that we've been dissatisfied with Northern Idaho, here in Northern Idaho, with our representation. So they would like to merge with us and form a separate state. Well, this isn't new, because we were going to do that 45 years ago. And we were going to call it the state of Lincoln. We had planned them. And people were really hot and bothered. And all of a sudden, the Seattle representatives started giving Spokane all kinds of considerations. And Southern Idaho bent over backwards to listen to us, because they didn't want to lose our mining tax dollars, and their lumbering tax dollars. So they really picked up their ears and realized that the people of northern Idaho was getting pretty ticked off about the way things were going. And we had hoped then that we were going to take everything from Lewiston to the Canadian border and make a separate state out of it, join in with Spokane. This isn't new. What they're talking about isn't new. It may yet happen if they keep on getting poor representation. Because we really got everything right here. We could use Spokane as an industrial area. We've got our mines for our mining industry. We've got our lumbering industry. We could be self-sustaining. And then on top of that, we always wanted gambling. And consequently, that's the reason why we wanted to get away from Southern Idaho, because they won't let us have gambling up here. And we would like very much to have gambling. We could have gambling and Coeur d'Alene could be another Reno real easy. If it happened, this is the kind of groundwork that could be laid to do it too because we've got all the power and strength that an area could use. The Finland Empire got it all right here. Then the little river boats would be pretty nice, wouldn't they? Oh, yeah. You could have nice cruises on the lake again, just like they used to. I got a brother-in-law that cruises people on Lake Tahoe. He said he's busy day after day. He makes good money doing that. You haven't seen those hooked up right on those? Yeah, they have. They take conventioneers, Boy Scout troops, Girl Scout troops, and everything. They give them wine and dine them on these big cruising ships that they got on the way. The ships that we had here, they went right up to St. Joe I noticed. They went back up into Cataldo there. Yeah, I think they did as far as Cataldo. See, they used to have a narrow gauge railroad that ran from Cataldo into Kellogg. And then they used to bring, in the early days, they used to bring their silver out from Kellogg and Wallace on that mail-and-faith railroad. And they'd put it on a barge, and then they'd barge it all the way down to Third Street here in Portland Lake. And then they'd take it in by train into Spokane to a refinery. Early days, that's how they used to do it. It was quite the transportation, wasn't it? Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. I understand they could, Ernie Wicks tipped over a barge of $60, 000 worth of silver off of McDonald Point. It's in about 90 feet of water. The guy come up here years ago. He wanted to know if I knew where it was. And I knew Ernie real well. He told me where he lost it. So I told him to go up there. And he goes, he found some samples of it, all right, but he said it's laying under about six feet of logs and silt and he said it would take an awful lot of work to get to it. You see it was $60, 000 then, so it's probably worth $300, 000 now. Yeah. But it's there. It's just a matter of how you're going to get to it. How did they tip it over? They got a guy in a storm and he was towing his barge And the storm started whipping his barge back and forth. And in order so that it didn't pull the boat down, he had to cut the barge loose. And he just let the storm take it. And it just started choking him. So it was big facts. And it would blow from one side to the other. Eventually it just dropped off in that channel all the way down there from McDonald Point to Point Mill Point. They had quite a transportation. I understand that on the Fond du Rez Lake, that they had those river boats going all the way to Missoula. Have they? Yeah. I see by the paper where they confiscated, they dug one of those boats up. It was sunk in the backwater someplace. And I see by the one of the Montana papers they dug that thing out. When I was in the hospital in Tocantins, kidney operation, one of my roommates' father was a pilot on a helicopter, his grandfather was. He said he knew where that was, too. Some of those old timers knew where they were, but nobody would go after it. So he just left it there. There's some around this area where we could, at first, we could still dig it up if they really wanted to. Or would it be worth even digging it up? Most of them are in Cloud Bay. They used to drift over Cloud Bay. There's five or six over there. As a matter of fact, when this water was real low here a couple of years ago, you could see the top of the paddle wheel of the big rope. You could see it from the top. And when the water was clear, you could look down and see it. How did they think they blew them up, or did they? Burn them up. Burn them up. So if you actually brought one up, it wouldn't even just scratch. It's what they call Swedish funeral. Swedish funeral. Yeah. Yeah. They'd see in Sweden that customary thing. Whenever a boat got real old or something like that, or somebody died, they would put their remains on the boat, set it on fire, and then let that others adrift. And then they'd let the extremist people on it and the boat itself. Around what period of time did they burn all these riverboats? Between 1920 and about 1935. I remember seeing one of them go down. I think it was I watched it and I'm fortunate to have a thumb drive. We were all sitting there, a bunch of us, just sitting there. It was funny, that thing broke out into a fire. Just about that time the whole thing went off. Was that right here on the Coeur d'Alene? Yeah, right there on the beach. Which one was that? I think that was the Miss Pocan. We had played on it before, but it was a few days before that. When did the river traffic stop around here? I think that was just about that same time that they were able to get the road around St. Mary's that they'd been, so after they got the road around there, they didn't need to go. You

lines.line: # A Lesson in Scorn

> The interviewee recounts a story about an affair in 1910 involving a barber's daughter. She discovers her husband's infidelity and eventually takes revenge by blowing up the covered wagon where her husband and his mistress meet. This event was notably aggressive, leading to the daughter's nervous breakdown.
** apprenticeship, affair, revenge, dynamite, nervous breakdown **
keywords: ** apprenticeship, affair, revenge, dynamite, nervous breakdown **

lines.line: # Trading and Fur Business

> A discussion on the trading practices of the past involving Native Americans at Black Ball Island, highlighting their trade of furs for bacon. Details about the American Fur Company and its representative, Steve Liberty, are shared.
** trading, Native Americans, furs, American Fur Company, Steve Liberty **

lines.line: # The Establishment of Fort Sherman

> Explains why Fort Sherman was established, primarily due to the Nez Perce wars. It was a measure to monitor the Coeur d'Alene Indians. The fort also provided protection to homesteaders, with a single sheriff unable to cover the large area.
** Fort Sherman, Nez Perce wars, Coeur d'Alene Indians, protection, army **
keywords: ** Fort Sherman, Nez Perce wars, Coeur d'Alene Indians, protection, army **

# Crime and Vice in Historical Coeur d’Alene
